description:
You are a FinOps analyst who has just received a raw usage-and-spend export from the cloud provider.  
Your goal: produce a concise JSON summary of total USD spend per cloud service.

Initial data location  
• CSV file: /home/user/cloud_costs/raw/march_2024_spend.csv  

Columns inside the CSV (comma-separated, header included, no extra whitespace):  
Date,Account,Service,CostUSD

Required work  
1. Read the CSV file and add up CostUSD for every unique value in the “Service” column.  
2. Create a new directory /home/user/cloud_costs/results if it does not yet exist.  
3. Write a JSON file named /home/user/cloud_costs/results/march_2024_service_totals.json that contains the totals.  
   • The file must be a single top-level JSON array.  
   • Each element must be an object with exactly two keys in this order:  
     1) "Service"  2) "TotalSpend"  
   • Values:  
     - "Service": the service name as it appears in the CSV.  
     - "TotalSpend": the summed cost rounded to exactly two decimal places (string quotes not required, keep it as a JSON number).  
   • The objects must be sorted alphabetically by the Service name.  
   • Example of the required formatting (four-space indentation, newline after each array item, final newline at EOF):
[
    {"Service": "Compute",    "TotalSpend": 40.50},
    {"Service": "Database",   "TotalSpend": 50.00},
    {"Service": "Networking", "TotalSpend": 21.00},
    {"Service": "Storage",    "TotalSpend": 18.00}
]

4. (Optional but recommended) keep a simple command log for your own records at  
   /home/user/cloud_costs/results/command_log.txt  
   Use one line per command, formatted exactly as:  
   StepN|the full command you executed

Success criteria (automated tests will check only the JSON file):  
• /home/user/cloud_costs/results/march_2024_service_totals.json exists.  
• It is valid JSON, contains a single array with objects ordered alphabetically by Service.  
• For each Service, TotalSpend matches the correct sum of the CostUSD values in the CSV, shown with exactly two digits after the decimal point.  
• File ends with a trailing newline.

You can use any command-line utilities available in a standard Ubuntu environment (e.g., awk, csvtool, jq, python) to accomplish the task. Keep the solution to roughly two to three commands if possible.
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the agent begins:

Directory structure already present
/home/user/cloud_costs
/home/user/cloud_costs/raw

File present
Path: /home/user/cloud_costs/raw/march_2024_spend.csv  
Contents (exact, including header and trailing newline):
Date,Account,Service,CostUSD
2024-03-01,Dev,Compute,15.50
2024-03-02,Prod,Compute,25.00
2024-03-03,Dev,Storage,5.25
2024-03-04,Prod,Storage,12.75
2024-03-05,Dev,Database,20.00
2024-03-06,Prod,Database,30.00
2024-03-07,Dev,Networking,7.00
2024-03-08,Prod,Networking,14.00

Correct values that the agent must compute
Compute   -> 40.50
Database  -> 50.00
Networking-> 21.00
Storage   -> 18.00

Expected output file once the task is complete
Path: /home/user/cloud_costs/results/march_2024_service_totals.json  
Contents (exact; note four spaces indentation, single space after colons, three spaces before the aligned numbers, final newline):
[
    {"Service": "Compute",    "TotalSpend": 40.50},
    {"Service": "Database",   "TotalSpend": 50.00},
    {"Service": "Networking", "TotalSpend": 21.00},
    {"Service": "Storage",    "TotalSpend": 18.00}
]

The automated grader will:
1. Verify that the JSON file exists at the listed path.
2. Parse the JSON and confirm:
   • It is an array with exactly four objects.
   • Keys are exactly "Service" and "TotalSpend" in that order.
   • Objects appear in alphabetical Service order.
   • Numeric values match the above totals to two decimal places.
3. Confirm the file ends with a single newline character.

command_log.txt (if created) is not graded but must be writable by the agent.

No environment variables or external network access are required.
